world-news.title: Iran war live: Tehran says diplomacy continues but no deal yet with US
world-news.summary: The Middle East is in crisis as Israeli military operations continue escalating in Lebanon, with over 3,100 people killed and 9,400 injured since March. Meanwhile, Iran and the US are engaged in delicate negotiations, but diplomacy has stalled with no agreement reached yet. This situation matters because the region is on the brink of a wider regional war involving multiple nations—Israel, Iran, Lebanon's Hezbollah militia, and the United States. The outcome will affect global oil prices, refugee movements, and international security. A full-scale conflict could drag in other countries and destabilize the entire Middle East, making this one of the most critical geopolitical crises happening right now.

world-news.title: Russia's Putin vows retaliation after accusing Ukraine of hitting student dormitory
world-news.summary: Russian President Vladimir Putin has promised to retaliate after claiming Ukrainian forces struck a student dormitory, escalating the ongoing war between Russia and Ukraine. Ukraine denies this and says it actually targeted a Russian Rubicon drone military unit in eastern Ukraine. This matters because Russia and Ukraine have been at war since 2022, and each side's military actions and accusations shape international support and weapons aid. Putin's retaliation threats suggest the conflict is intensifying, which affects global energy prices, food supplies, and could draw NATO (the Western military alliance) into direct conflict with Russia. Understanding this war is crucial because it's reshaping global politics and economics daily.

world-news.title: Rubio tries to reassure NATO allies over US troop deployments
world-news.summary: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio is working to calm concerns from NATO allies (Western countries in a military alliance) after President Trump announced plans to send more troops to Poland. This comes after Trump's team cancelled a similar troop deployment just a week earlier, creating confusion about American military commitment. This matters because NATO countries in Eastern Europe—especially Poland—rely on US military protection against Russian threats. If America seems unreliable, these countries might feel vulnerable and seek nuclear weapons or make independent deals with Russia, destabilizing Europe. Rubio's reassurance mission shows Trump's unpredictable foreign policy is creating diplomatic challenges with traditional American allies.

world-news.title: Ebola risk raised to 'very high' in DR Congo
world-news.summary: The United Nations health agency has raised the Ebola risk level to 'very high' in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), a country in Central Africa where the virus is spreading. While global risk remains 'low,' the situation is serious in the DRC and surrounding regions because Ebola is deadly—it kills up to 90% of infected people. This matters because outbreaks can spread across borders through travel and trade, potentially becoming a global health crisis like COVID-19. The DRC outbreak threatens neighboring Uganda and could strain international health resources. Health organizations are mobilizing vaccines and medical teams to contain the disease before it spreads further, making this a critical global health emergency to monitor.

world-news.title: Bolivia in crisis: Social unrest, demands for president to resign escalate
world-news.summary: Bolivia, a South American country, is experiencing major political and social instability as protests intensify and citizens are demanding President Rodrigo Paz resign. This situation reflects deeper problems including economic hardship, inequality, and lack of faith in government leadership. This matters globally because political instability in Latin America can trigger humanitarian crises, migration surges to neighboring countries and the US, and create opportunities for authoritarian leaders to seize power. When governments become unstable, it also affects international trade and investment in the region. Bolivia's crisis shows how economic inequality and weak institutions can destabilize entire nations, with ripple effects felt across the Americas.

world-news.title: Ebola outbreak disrupts life along the DRC-Uganda border
world-news.summary: Nearly 750 suspected Ebola cases have been reported in the Democratic Republic of Congo, and the outbreak is now disrupting daily life along the border with Uganda, a neighboring country. Health officials have intensified screening and prevention measures at the border to prevent the virus from crossing into Uganda and beyond. This matters because borders between African nations are often porous—people cross regularly for work, family, and trade—making disease containment extremely difficult. If Ebola spreads to Uganda and other neighboring countries, it could become a regional epidemic affecting millions. International organizations are working to contain the outbreak through vaccination campaigns and quarantine measures, but success depends on cross-border cooperation and adequate funding.

world-news.title: Gaza flotilla activists allege abuse by Israeli forces while detained
world-news.summary: Activists aboard a flotilla (a group of ships) attempting to deliver aid to Gaza claim they were abused by Israeli forces during detention, including being slapped, hit, and subjected to sexual violence. Israel's prison service denies these allegations, creating a dispute over what actually happened. This matters because Gaza is a Palestinian territory under Israeli blockade, and humanitarian aid is a major geopolitical issue. The Israeli-Palestinian conflict is one of the world's longest-running disputes, affecting Middle East stability and international relations. Accusations of abuse by Israeli forces fuel anger among Palestinians and their supporters worldwide, potentially escalating tensions and violence in an already volatile region.

world-news.title: Carney says Alberta is 'essential' to Canada as province plans vote on separation
world-news.summary: Canada's federal government is warning that Alberta, a wealthy oil-producing province, is essential to the country's stability after Alberta announced plans to hold a referendum on separation (secession is when a region leaves a country to become independent). Canada's top official says Alberta is crucial to the nation's economy and unity, urging the province to reconsider. This matters because if Alberta separated, Canada would lose vast oil and gas resources, weakening its economy and geopolitical influence. Separatism in wealthy regions challenges national unity and can inspire other regions to demand independence. This situation reflects rising tensions between resource-rich provinces and the federal government over resource control, taxation, and representation in Canadian politics.

world-news.title: Mahmoud Khalil to appeal US deportation case to Supreme Court
world-news.summary: Mahmoud Khalil, a pro-Palestine advocate, is appealing his deportation case to the US Supreme Court, arguing that the Trump administration targeted him for his protected free speech and political activism. This case raises important questions about whether the US government can deport people based on their political beliefs rather than criminal activity. This matters because free speech and political expression are fundamental rights in democracies, and using deportation as punishment for activism threatens those freedoms. The case also highlights tensions between immigration enforcement and civil liberties, and reflects broader debates about government overreach. The Supreme Court's decision will set precedent for how the US handles deportations of political activists.

world-news.title: Tulsi Gabbard to resign as US director of national intelligence
world-news.summary: Tulsi Gabbard, who serves as the US Director of National Intelligence (the top official overseeing America's spy agencies), is resigning from her position due to her husband's illness. Gabbard has been largely absent from public view during recent US military operations, raising questions about her engagement in this critical national security role. This matters because the Director of National Intelligence is responsible for coordinating all US spy agencies and advising the President on national security threats. Her departure creates uncertainty about continuity in US intelligence operations during a period of global instability involving Russia, Iran, and China. The resignation also raises questions about leadership stability within the Trump administration during critical geopolitical crises.

finance-markets.title: Wall Street bets on 2026 rate rise as Kevin Warsh takes charge of the Fed
finance-markets.summary: Kevin Warsh, a prominent financial expert, is taking leadership of the Federal Reserve—basically America's bank that controls interest rates (the cost of borrowing money). Wall Street investors are betting the Fed will raise interest rates by the end of 2026, meaning loans will become more expensive for borrowing. This matters because higher rates affect everything: credit cards, car loans, mortgages, and even your parents' savings. The timing is tricky because conflict with Iran could push up prices (inflation), forcing the Fed to act faster. Understanding the Fed is crucial since it influences whether you'll pay more or less to borrow money for college or a future home.

finance-markets.title: Trump administration to make foreigners leave US to apply for green cards
finance-markets.summary: The Trump administration is changing rules about green cards—permanent residency permits that allow foreign workers to live and work legally in America long-term. The new policy requires foreigners currently in the US to leave the country to apply, rather than applying while already here. This directly impacts businesses that rely on immigrant workers, from tech companies to farms, because it creates delays and uncertainty in hiring. For teenagers, this matters because it affects the job market, could influence what careers are available, and shows how government policy shapes where companies operate. Companies are worried about losing skilled workers, which could slow economic growth and innovation.

finance-markets.title: Nasdaq Gets Approval From SEC to List Bitcoin Index Options
finance-markets.summary: The SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission—the government agency that oversees stock markets) just approved Bitcoin options on the Nasdaq exchange, one of America's biggest stock markets. Bitcoin is a digital currency that exists only online, and 'options' are financial contracts letting people bet on price changes without owning the actual asset. This approval is huge because it means Wall Street's biggest institutions are now integrating with cryptocurrency more deeply, making digital assets more mainstream and legitimate. For teenagers interested in investing or finance, this signals that crypto is becoming less fringe and more integrated into traditional finance. However, it also means more risk if something goes wrong, since cryptocurrency is still relatively new and unpredictable compared to traditional stocks.

finance-markets.title: Tulsi Gabbard resigns as Donald Trump's US intelligence chief
finance-markets.summary: Tulsi Gabbard, who was leading America's intelligence agencies (the organizations that gather secret information about threats to national security), has resigned from her position. Her departure comes as President Trump weighs whether to launch military strikes on Iran, a major decision that intelligence leaders typically influence. Gabbard was a controversial pick because some questioned her judgment on foreign policy, so her exit signals turmoil in the Trump administration's national security team. This matters because intelligence leadership affects which foreign policy decisions get made—wars, sanctions (financial punishments), and diplomatic deals all depend on accurate intelligence. When key officials resign mid-crisis, it usually means disagreements about strategy, which can affect global stability and markets.

finance-markets.title: Emerging Assets Post Positive Week as Hopes for Peace Deal Grow
finance-markets.summary: Stocks and currencies from emerging markets (developing countries with growing economies like Brazil, India, and Mexico) gained value this week because investors are increasingly hopeful that the US and Iran might reach a peace deal. Emerging markets are sensitive to geopolitical conflict because war disrupts trade and increases uncertainty—when peace seems possible, investors rush back in, pushing prices up. This is important because emerging markets represent the future of global growth; they're where many teenagers will find jobs in coming decades. When these markets do well, it signals global optimism and stability. Conversely, when they struggle, it often means serious global problems are brewing that could affect everyone's jobs and economic opportunities worldwide.

finance-markets.title: Pakistan army chief travels to Tehran in push for US-Iran deal
finance-markets.summary: Asim Munir, Pakistan's army chief (the military leader of a nuclear-armed nation of 230+ million people), traveled to Tehran (Iran's capital) to help negotiate peace between the US and Iran. He's joining other mediators like Qatar in trying to prevent a full-scale war. Pakistan's involvement matters because it's geographically close to both Iran and Afghanistan, making it a crucial player in Middle East stability and trade routes. A US-Iran conflict would devastate global oil prices, supply chains, and economies worldwide—teenager's families would feel it at the gas pump and grocery store. Pakistan's presence signals serious diplomatic efforts, though it also shows how fragile peace negotiations are when even military leaders must personally intervene to prevent war.

finance-markets.title: Grocery Stores Feel Crunch of Fuel, Labor Costs Ahead of Memorial Day
finance-markets.summary: Stew Leonard Jr., who runs a major grocery store chain, explains that customers are buying food for holiday celebrations but complaining about rising prices in the store. The squeeze comes from two sources: fuel costs (for transporting food) and labor costs (paying workers enough to attract and retain them). While Leonard hopes fuel prices drop once Middle East conflict resolves, labor costs will stay high, meaning grocery prices will likely keep rising. This directly affects your family's budget—it's why your parents might complain about expensive groceries. Understanding this connection between geopolitical conflict, supply chains, and everyday prices helps you see how global events impact your daily life, from what you eat to how much money your family spends on basics.

finance-markets.title: Trump's risky military options to break Iran deadlock
finance-markets.summary: President Trump is considering several military options against Iran—from air strikes to naval operations—to force Iran to negotiate or surrender. There's no simple military solution because Iran is a large, well-armed country with allies, so any attack could trigger a wider regional war affecting global oil supplies and economies. The 'deadlock' refers to the standoff where neither side wants to back down, yet both are unwilling to fully commit to war. This is crucial for teenagers because war decisions made now could affect the economy for years—oil prices, inflation, job markets, and military spending all depend on these choices. It also shows how modern presidents face impossible choices where every option carries massive risks for millions of people worldwide.

finance-markets.title: If you think you understand bonds, you don't
finance-markets.summary: This article explains that bonds—basically IOUs where you loan money to governments or companies and they pay you back with interest—are far more complex than most people realize. The article warns about five common mistakes people make when investing in bonds, though it doesn't detail them all here. Bonds matter because they're how governments and companies borrow trillions of dollars, so bond markets affect interest rates, inflation, and entire economies. As a teenager, understanding bonds is important because they're likely in your parents' retirement accounts, and bond prices affect how much money they'll have for retirement. Plus, when you eventually borrow for college or a home, interest rates on those loans are linked to bond markets, making this knowledge practically relevant to your future finances.

finance-markets.title: Lantheus Explores Potential $7 Billion Sale Following Offer From Curium
finance-markets.summary: Lantheus Holdings, a major radiopharma company (a business making radioactive medicines for medical imaging and cancer treatment), is considering selling itself after receiving a $7 billion takeover offer from Curium, another pharmaceutical company. When companies this size explore sales, it signals either financial struggles or attractive valuations that make selling more lucrative than staying independent. This matters for healthcare because company mergers can affect drug prices, research investment, and patient access to medicines. For teenagers considering healthcare careers, consolidation in pharma means fewer independent companies hiring but often better resources at larger merged entities. These kinds of deals also show how financial markets work—successful companies either grow independently or get acquired, reshaping entire industries and affecting which companies will exist and compete in your future.

science-space.title: SpaceX just launched Starship V3 — its most powerful megarocket yet — into space for the 1st time in spectacular Flight 12 test
science-space.summary: SpaceX, the private space company founded by Elon Musk, just successfully launched Starship V3, the most powerful rocket ever built, marking a major milestone in space exploration. This wasn't SpaceX's first try—they've been testing Starship for years, learning from each failed or partial test flight to make the next one better. Why this matters: Starship is designed to carry astronauts to the Moon and eventually Mars, which means humanity could finally establish permanent settlements on other planets. This test flight shows we're getting closer to making interplanetary travel a reality. For teenagers, this represents the future of space careers and exploration you might participate in.

science-space.title: New Material Could Help NASA Melt Moon Rocks, Harness Lunar Resources
science-space.summary: NASA researchers at Glenn Research Center in Cleveland have discovered a special new material that could revolutionize how astronauts explore the Moon. Instead of carrying everything they need from Earth (which is extremely expensive and heavy), astronauts could use this material to melt Moon rocks and extract valuable resources like metals and oxygen. Why this matters: If astronauts can 'live off the land' on the Moon by using local materials, future missions become cheaper, lighter, and more sustainable. This means we could build permanent Moon bases and use them as launching points for Mars missions. For you, this represents the kind of innovation that makes deep space exploration actually possible rather than just a dream.

science-space.title: NASA is updating its Artemis moon base plan. You can find out how on May 26.
science-space.summary: NASA is about to reveal major updates to its Artemis program, which is the agency's ambitious plan to return humans to the Moon and establish a permanent base there. Artemis is named after the twin sister of Apollo in Greek mythology, mirroring the original Apollo Moon missions of the 1960s and 1970s. Why this matters: This update will tell us exactly how NASA plans to build infrastructure on the Moon, who will go, and when missions will launch. Understanding these plans helps us see the timeline for when regular people (not just astronauts) might travel to space. The livestream on May 26 means anyone can watch NASA's leaders explain humanity's near-future in space exploration, making it accessible and exciting for everyone.

science-space.title: NASA to Compete Contract for Jet Propulsion Laboratory Management
science-space.summary: NASA announced it will open up management of the Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) in Southern California to competition for the first time since the laboratory was founded. JPL is NASA's most famous research center, responsible for designing and controlling many space missions including rovers on Mars and the James Webb Space Telescope. Currently, Caltech (California Institute of Technology) has run JPL since its creation nearly a century ago. Why this matters: When NASA 'competes' a contract, it means other organizations can bid to take over JPL's management, which could bring new ideas and ensure taxpayer money is spent efficiently. This is a huge deal because JPL has been operating under the same management for nearly 100 years, so change could mean new approaches to space exploration. For teenagers interested in space careers, this could mean JPL becomes even more innovative and exciting.

science-space.title: Webb Studies Star Clusters
science-space.summary: NASA's James Webb Space Telescope, the most powerful space observatory ever built, has completed a massive study of nearly 9,000 star clusters across four nearby galaxies, including the famous Messier 51 galaxy. A star cluster is a group of thousands or millions of stars bound together by gravity, and studying them helps scientists understand how stars form and evolve. The new data reveals that more massive star clusters tend to form and survive in certain galactic environments. Why this matters: Understanding star clusters teaches us about the universe's history and the physical laws governing star formation, knowledge that could help us predict where to find habitable planets. The James Webb Telescope represents humanity's most advanced eye on the cosmos, capable of seeing light from over 13 billion years ago. These discoveries show how space exploration expands human knowledge in ways that affect everything from our understanding of physics to potential future space colonization.

science-space.title: An ancient solar storm left clues in tree rings and a famous poet's diary: 'Red lights in the northern sky'
science-space.summary: Scientists discovered evidence of a massive solar storm that occurred centuries ago by combining two unexpected sources: tree rings from around the world and a diary entry from a Japanese poet who witnessed unusual red lights in Kyoto's sky. A solar storm happens when the Sun releases enormous bursts of energy that can damage satellites and power grids today, but centuries ago, people simply saw strange auroras. By analyzing tree rings (which show carbon-14 levels that spike during solar storms), researchers confirmed what the poet observed. Why this matters: Understanding historical solar storms helps scientists predict future ones, which is critical because a major solar storm today could knock out power grids and communications satellites for months. This detective work shows how scientists use creative methods—combining history, poetry, and nature—to solve mysteries about our universe. For teenagers, it demonstrates that space science connects to history, literature, and practical technology affecting your daily life.

science-space.title: NASA Sets Coverage for Roscosmos Spacewalk Outside Space Station
science-space.summary: Two Russian cosmonauts (Russia's version of astronauts) from the space agency Roscosmos will conduct a spacewalk—a dangerous mission where astronauts leave their spacecraft and float in the vacuum of space—outside the International Space Station on May 27. The spacewalk will last approximately five hours and NASA is providing live coverage so the public can watch. Roscosmos is Russia's federal space agency, similar to how NASA serves the United States. Why this matters: Spacewalks are essential for maintaining and upgrading the International Space Station, humanity's only permanent laboratory in space where astronauts from different countries work together. Watching a live spacewalk shows the incredible danger and skill required for space exploration—astronauts are essentially working in the harshest environment imaginable with just a suit protecting them. This event demonstrates international cooperation in space despite geopolitical tensions on Earth, showing that science and exploration can unite humanity.

science-space.title: NASA Announces Realignment to Accelerate Mission Delivery
science-space.summary: NASA announced a major internal restructuring designed to help the agency accomplish its space exploration goals faster and more efficiently. This realignment represents leadership's strategy to organize NASA's various centers and departments differently so they can focus more directly on completing missions like Moon exploration and Mars missions. The changes align with the National Space Policy, which is the U.S. government's official plan for space activities. Why this matters: When large organizations like NASA reorganize, it usually means they're responding to new priorities or trying to fix inefficiencies. In this case, NASA is essentially saying 'we need to move faster on our biggest missions.' For teenagers interested in NASA careers, organizational changes like this can create new opportunities in different departments. Understanding that even space agencies constantly evolve and improve shows that innovation isn't just about technology—it's also about how organizations manage themselves to achieve ambitious goals.

science-space.title: Hubble Captures Galaxy Cluster
science-space.summary: NASA's Hubble Space Telescope captured a stunning image of a galaxy cluster—a gravitationally bound collection of hundreds or thousands of galaxies clustered together in space. The image shows galaxies of various shapes and sizes, with some being spiral-shaped like our Milky Way and others being elliptical. The bright spikes extending from some stars are called diffraction spikes, an optical effect created by the telescope's design. Why this matters: Galaxy clusters help scientists understand dark matter and gravity on cosmic scales, leading to breakthrough discoveries about how the universe formed and evolved. The Hubble Telescope has been humanity's primary eye on the universe since 1990, making discoveries that fundamentally changed our understanding of cosmology (the study of the universe's origin and structure). For teenagers, these images are more than just pretty pictures—they represent real scientific data helping answer humanity's biggest questions about our place in the cosmos.

science-space.title: Call for Creatives: NASA Seeks Help Illuminating Mission Storytelling
science-space.summary: NASA is launching an initiative inviting filmmakers, documentarians, songwriters, poets, and other creative professionals to help tell the stories of NASA missions in innovative ways. This includes missions like Artemis (returning to the Moon), nuclear propulsion research, and aeronautics (airplane) innovations. NASA believes that space exploration isn't just about scientists and engineers—it also needs artists and storytellers to help the public understand and get excited about these missions. Why this matters: Space exploration funding depends partly on public support, and the public becomes interested when they can emotionally connect with missions through compelling stories, films, and music. This invitation shows NASA recognizes that science communication through art is just as important as the science itself. For creative teenagers, this represents a career path where artistic talent meets space exploration—you could combine your passion for filmmaking, music, or writing with humanity's quest to explore the cosmos.

tech-ai.title: We tried Google's AI glasses and they're almost there
tech-ai.summary: Google just showed off prototype Android XR glasses that could change how we interact with the world around us. These aren't ordinary glasses — they use Gemini (Google's AI assistant) to overlay information directly into your field of view, kind of like having a personal AI guide following you everywhere. Imagine looking at street signs and instantly seeing translations in your language, or getting navigation directions projected right in front of your eyes without checking your phone. This is significant because it represents the next frontier of computing: moving away from screens and into wearable technology. If successful, these glasses could become as essential as smartphones, making AI assistance something you literally wear. The technology isn't perfect yet, but Google's working toward making science fiction real in the near future.

tech-ai.title: SpaceX launches Starship V3 for the first time, but loses booster on return
tech-ai.summary: SpaceX, the space company founded by Elon Musk, just launched its upgraded Starship V3 rocket — a massive vehicle designed to carry cargo and eventually humans to space and other planets. While the launch itself went mostly smoothly, something went wrong during the booster's return to Earth: SpaceX lost the booster (the rocket's first stage that helps launch it into space). Think of a booster like a reusable rocket engine that typically parachutes back down so SpaceX can use it again, saving money. This launch matters because Starship is central to SpaceX's ambitious goals, including missions to Mars, the Moon, and building a fully reusable space transportation system. Even though they lost the booster this time, successfully launching an upgraded Starship is a huge step forward in making space travel more accessible and affordable for humanity's future.

tech-ai.title: AI is being used to resurrect the voices of dead pilots
tech-ai.summary: Researchers used artificial intelligence to reconstruct the voices of deceased pilots by analyzing spectrograms — visual representations of cockpit audio recordings. A spectrogram is basically a digital fingerprint of sound that shows what different voices sound like. This development is raising serious concerns about how AI can be misused: if we can recreate someone's voice after they've died, what prevents bad actors from impersonating people to commit fraud or spread misinformation? The discovery was so concerning that the NTSB (National Transportation Safety Board, the government agency that investigates plane crashes) temporarily shut down public access to its database of cockpit recordings to protect pilot privacy. This story matters because it highlights both the incredible power of AI technology and the urgent need for safeguards. We need to figure out ethical rules for this technology before it becomes common and gets abused.

tech-ai.title: You can no longer Google the word 'disregard'
tech-ai.summary: Google's newest AI search feature — called AI Overviews — has a weird glitch: if you search for the word 'disregard,' the search results break. Instead of showing you a helpful summary of what that word means, Google's AI acts like a chatbot and responds with something like 'Got it. If you need anything else, let me know!' This is embarrassing for Google because it shows their AI system is confused and not working as intended. Google's AI Overviews are supposed to give you quick answers at the top of search results, but this bug demonstrates how AI systems can fail in unexpected ways when they encounter unusual inputs. The problem got so bad that Google had to fix it. This matters because billions of people rely on Google Search daily, and if AI features aren't reliable, it undermines trust in the entire platform. It's a reminder that even massive tech companies are still figuring out how to make AI work correctly.

tech-ai.title: Apple says Epic lawsuit shouldn't reshape App Store rules for all developers
tech-ai.summary: Apple is fighting a legal battle with Epic Games (the company behind the video game Fortnite) over control of the App Store — Apple's digital marketplace where people download apps and games. Epic Games won a court ruling that forces Apple to allow other payment methods besides Apple's own payment system, which charges companies fees for each transaction. Now Apple is asking the Supreme Court (the highest court in the US) to narrow that ruling so it only applies to Epic, not all app developers. Essentially, Apple wants to keep its strict control over how apps operate and make money on iPhones while avoiding having to change rules for everyone else. This matters because it affects whether you'll ever have more choices for how to buy digital content on your iPhone, and whether Apple's control over its ecosystem is fair. The outcome could reshape how all tech companies manage their platforms and what rules they must follow.

tech-ai.title: How VCs and founders use inflated 'ARR' to crown AI startups
tech-ai.summary: Some AI startup companies are exaggerating their revenue numbers when talking publicly about their success, and venture capitalists (VCs) — wealthy investors who fund new companies — are aware it's happening. ARR stands for 'Annual Recurring Revenue,' which is the money a company expects to make from customers each year. The problem is that some startups are counting money they haven't actually received yet, or inflating numbers in misleading ways, to make their businesses look more successful than they really are. Their investors know about these inflated numbers but go along with it anyway because AI startups are extremely trendy right now and everyone wants to invest in the next big thing. This matters because it means the AI startup boom might be built partly on hype rather than real business success. If these companies collapse when investors realize the numbers were fake, it could damage the entire AI industry's credibility and waste billions of dollars that could have gone to legitimate innovations.

tech-ai.title: Waymo suspends freeway driving amid safety concerns
tech-ai.summary: Waymo, the autonomous vehicle company owned by Google's parent company Alphabet, just disabled freeway driving across all its US markets due to safety concerns about construction zones — areas where roads are being repaired or rebuilt. Construction zones are dangerous and unpredictable because road layouts change constantly, barriers move, and workers operate nearby. Waymo also paused service entirely in Atlanta and San Antonio after problems with flooded roads. Autonomous vehicles (self-driving cars) rely on AI and sensors to navigate, but construction zones present unique challenges that their AI systems apparently aren't confident handling yet. This suspension matters because it shows that despite impressive progress, self-driving cars still have significant limitations and safety concerns that need solving before they can reliably operate everywhere. It's a realistic reminder that deploying AI in real-world transportation requires extreme caution, and companies must pause operations when safety is uncertain rather than risk harming people.

tech-ai.title: Meta's Forum is part Reddit, part Facebook, and part Google AI Overview
tech-ai.summary: Meta (Facebook's parent company) just released a new app called Forum for iPhones that combines Facebook Groups with an AI chatbot to help users find information and get advice. Think of it like this: instead of asking a question on Reddit or searching Google, you can now ask in your Facebook groups and get both answers from real people and suggestions from Meta's AI system. The app automatically pulls in all your existing Facebook groups when you sign in, making it easy to browse discussions and post questions. Meta is trying to compete with other platforms where people ask questions online — Reddit (a discussion forum) and Google Search (where people add 'Reddit' to get real human answers instead of AI-generated ones). This matters because Meta is betting that people want to ask questions within their existing social networks rather than on random websites. It shows how major tech companies are scrambling to integrate AI into existing services and keep users engaged with their platforms.

tech-ai.title: Google appeals search monopoly ruling, says it won business 'fair and square'
tech-ai.summary: Google officially appealed a major federal court ruling that declared Google an illegal search monopoly — meaning the government determined Google unfairly dominates the search market and blocks competition. In its appeal, Google argues it won the search market 'fair and square' through better products, not illegal practices, and claims the court's decision 'crashed' through legal guardrails and boundaries. A monopoly is when one company controls a market so completely that competitors can't compete fairly; a search monopoly means Google has such overwhelming dominance that other search engines struggle to survive. This case matters enormously because if Google loses on appeal, the government might force it to share data with competitors or even break up the company. The outcome will determine whether giant tech companies can continue controlling entire markets or whether the government will enforce stricter competition rules. It's fundamentally about how much power a single company should have in the digital world.

tech-ai.title: Blue Origin cleared to fly New Glenn mega-rocket after April mishap
tech-ai.summary: Blue Origin, the aerospace company founded by Jeff Bezos (Amazon's founder), has been cleared to resume flying its New Glenn mega-rocket after an engine failure in April that destroyed a satellite. An engine failure means one of the rocket's powerful motors didn't work properly, causing the launch to fail and resulting in the loss of an AST SpaceMobile satellite (a device designed to provide cellular service from space). The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) gave Blue Origin permission to launch again after investigating what went wrong, though the company provided limited details about the failure. This matters because Blue Origin is one of the major competitors in the commercial space industry alongside SpaceX, and setbacks like this delay important satellite launches and space projects. The fact that they've been cleared to fly again shows the rocket industry has systems in place to investigate failures and improve safety before restarting operations. It demonstrates how space technology is advancing through trial, error, and continuous improvement.

Hundreds of scientists gathered in London this week to discuss the role of migration as...

energy-climate.summary:
Hundreds of scientists in dozens of institutions are embarking on the next phase of the...

Investing in flood defences, air conditioning and other measures to protect the UK from climate...

energy-climate.title: Factcheck: Trump’s false claims about the IPCC and ‘RCP8.5’ climate scenario
energy-climate.summary:

energy-climate.title: China invalidates First Solar TOPCon patent in major win for JinkoSolar
energy-climate.summary: JinkoSolar has won a key patent invalidation in China against First Solar’s TOPCon-related patent, with the China National Intellectual Property Administration ruling it fully invalid.

energy-climate.title: EBRD lends €70 million to Slovenian BESS developer across four countries
energy-climate.summary: The European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD) is backing a five-country merchant battery storage push by Slovenian developer NGEN Energetske Rešitve with a €70 million ($81.3 million) loan supported by an EU first-loss guarantee, in one of the first multi-country merchant BESS portfolios in central Europe.

energy-climate.title: ‘I still remember an HR director guessing my age during a recruitment process and assuming I would soon have children’
energy-climate.summary: This week Women in Solar+ Europe gives voice to Marián Giner, Head of Engineering at Sweden's Alight. She speaks about systemic barriers, impostor syndrome, and lessons from sport and mentorship to redefine leadership as collaborative, transparent, and team-focused.

energy-climate.title: Chinese PV Industry Brief: Polysilicon prices remain flat amid supply-demand rebalancing signals
energy-climate.summary: Polysilicon prices stayed flat in China as production and demand moved closer into balance, easing recent supply–demand pressures.

energy-climate.title: Slovakia plans to develop energy communities
energy-climate.summary: Director of the Slovak Association of Sustainable Energy, Ján Karaba, told pv magazine solar is expected to become one of the key technologies used within future energy communities in Slovakia but said regulatory improvements will be essential to unlock the full potential of decentralized solar energy.

asia-pacific.title: 27 countries seek access to World Bank funds since Iran war
asia-pacific.summary:
Twenty-seven countries have moved since the Iran war started to put in place crisis instruments that could quickly access funding from existing World Bank programmes, according to an internal document viewed by Reuters.
The World Bank document did not name the ‌countries or the total amount of funds potentially being sought. The World Bank declined to comment.
The document showed that three countries had approved new instruments since the Middle East conflict began on February 28, while the...

asia-pacific.title: Japanese female prison’s rehabilitation goal faces health, language barriers
asia-pacific.summary:
Seated in a wheelchair, an elderly woman bends silently as her wrinkled fingers move with surprising speed to fold pieces of coloured origami paper into intricate shapes.
Beside her, another woman does the same, both adding their finished pieces to a pile on the table without looking up or exchanging a word.
Inside Tochigi Prison, where talking during work is forbidden, elderly women folding paper and sewing in silence reflect a broader test of Japan’s penal system: how to care for ageing,...

asia-pacific.title: How China wins by avoiding war
asia-pacific.summary:
Russian President Vladimir Putin arrived in Beijing in a much weaker position than he did more than four years ago, when he launched his invasion of Ukraine shortly after his last visit.
US President Donald Trump left Beijing after much fanfare. However, he was criticised back at home for failing to deliver on any key aspect of American interests, such as committing China to pressuring Iran to end the war on US terms. His popular support is at a historic low.
Putin, Trump and Chinese President..

asia-pacific.title: Hong Kong’s first astronaut Lai Ka-ying picked for space station mission
asia-pacific.summary:
Hong Kong will send its first astronaut to the Tiangong space station, the China Manned Space Agency announced on Saturday.
Lai Ka-ying, a police chief inspector and tech expert with a doctorate in computer science, will join the Shenzhou-23 mission as a payload specialist. She will become the fourth female astronaut to enter the Chinese space station.
The Shenzhou-23 crewed spacecraft is targeting a launch at 23:08 tomorrow.
According to the agency, she will work alongside astronauts Zhu...

asia-pacific.title: A cloud above the clouds: US, China race to make space a computing platform
asia-pacific.summary:
In 1957, the Soviet Union kicked off the Cold War’s space race, shocking the world by sending Sputnik, the world’s first artificial satellite, into orbit.
Four years later, it did the same to cosmonaut Yuri Gagarin, making him the first human in space. In 1969, the US provided another image that defined the era: Neil Armstrong leaving the Eagle lunar lander and planting the American flag on the moon, declaring it “one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.”

asia-pacific.title: Ancient Chinese texts depict ‘immortal mirror’ akin to CT scan – an early notion of robots
asia-pacific.summary:
Ancient China dreamed of future technology long before the modern age, conjuring visions of magic mirrors, ingenious machines and even lifelike wooden puppets.
In the Tang dynasty (618–907) text Duyang Miscellaneous Records, a mysterious stone called the “immortal mirror” appears as a precursor to the CT scan.
Found in the southwest of an unnamed land, the polished stone was said to span hundreds of kilometres.

Passers-by could look into it like a mirror, but instead of reflecting their faces,.

asia-pacific.title: Graduates of top vocational school spared brunt of AI impact, new chairman says
asia-pacific.summary:
Graduates of Hong Kong’s largest vocational training institution have largely avoided the negative effects of AI and could reap more internship opportunities from a coming megaproject, the body’s new leader has said.
In an interview with the South China Morning Post, newly appointed Vocational Training Council (VTC) chairman Jeffrey Lam Kin-fung also vowed to review the courses to align them with emerging job opportunities.
The government earlier revealed that entry-level jobs for university...

asia-pacific.title: Southeast Asia is chasing the AI boom, but at what cost?
asia-pacific.summary:
In a swanky penthouse office outside Kuala Lumpur, tech firm Zetrix AI is putting a grand plan into motion to get 1 million people across Southeast Asia using its artificial intelligence agent, Avatar, by the end of the year.
Designed as an autonomous alter ego, Avatar is adept at dealing with tasks ranging from the mundane, like filling out forms, to the particular, such as helping influencers reply to thousands of social media messages.
The ultimate goal, according to the company’s Gen Z...

asia-pacific.title: SpaceX launches its biggest, most beefed-up Starship yet on test flight
asia-pacific.summary:
SpaceX launched its biggest, most powerful Starship yet on a test flight Friday, an upgraded version that Nasa is counting on to land astronauts on the moon.
The redesigned mega rocket made its debut two days after SpaceX CEO Elon Musk announced he was taking the company public. It blasted off from the southern tip of Texas, carrying 20 mock Starlink satellites for release halfway around the world.
It is the 12th test flight of the rocket that Musk is building to get people to Mars one day. But.

asia-pacific.title: US judge tosses case against wrongly deported Salvadorean man Abrego Garcia
asia-pacific.summary:
A US federal judge dismissed a criminal case on Friday filed against a Salvadorean man at the centre of a row over US President Donald Trump’s crackdown on migrants.
US District Judge Waverly Crenshaw ruled that the indictment of Kilmar Abrego Garcia by the Trump administration’s Justice Department was “an abuse of prosecuting power”.
Abrego Garcia, a Maryland resident married to an American woman, was among more than 200 people sent to El Salvador’s CECOT mega-prison in March of last year.
